skill.name: parallel-swarm-implementation
skill.description: Coordinates parallel implementation work by turning a validated Loop 1 plan into agent assignments, execution groups, quality checks, and delivery artifacts.
skill.summary: Compile validated implementation plans into coordinated agent workflows with quality validation.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but the C2, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance hits are false positives from documentation and diagram text. Confirmed risks are medium: the skill asks agents to run shell commands, process project artifacts, and write local .claude files as part of orchestration. No malicious intent, exfiltration endpoint, prompt-injection override, or credential theft evidence was found.

medium_findings.title: Shell-Based Orchestration Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill instructs users or agents to run shell commands such as test, jq, npx claude-flow, npm test, and node scripts. These commands are part of the stated orchestration workflow, but they can execute project scripts and process artifact content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and numerous. They appear operational rather than malicious, so the risk is execution exposure, not confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Artifact-Controlled Data Enters Agent Instructions
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill reads planning and assignment artifacts, then injects their values into Task prompts and command strings. A malicious or compromised artifact could steer downstream agents unless reviewed or constrained.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from local artifacts into prompts is clear. Exploitability depends on whether the artifacts are trusted and how agents execute generated instructions.

medium_findings.title: Scoped Hidden Artifact File Access
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill reads and writes hidden .claude artifact files for coordination and delivery packages. The access is scoped to documented workflow files, but it still modifies local workspace state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and write calls are explicit. The paths are workflow-local, so this is a controlled filesystem risk rather than broad file access.

low_findings.title: C2 Keyword Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The DOT file references MCP server tools and graph edges in a process diagram. I found no command-and-control endpoint, beaconing behavior, remote host, or data exfiltration logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are diagram labels and Graphviz edges. They do not describe network callbacks or executable behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged locations contain descriptive planning text, JWT examples, graph labels, and skill names. I found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptographic implementation in these files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual context shows prose and workflow examples, not weak crypto APIs. The JWT example discusses token validation rather than implementing cryptography.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines refer to testing scenarios, dependency checks, progress updates, troubleshooting, or assignment validation. I found no host scanning, port scanning, network probing, or system inventory collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The terms occur in benign workflow and testing context. There is no evidence of reconnaissance commands or network targets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell-Based Orchestration Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill instructs users or agents to run shell commands such as test, jq, npx claude-flow, npm test, and node scripts. These commands are part of the stated orchestration workflow, but they can execute project scripts and process artifact content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and numerous. They appear operational rather than malicious, so the risk is execution exposure, not confirmed abuse.

dangerous_patterns.title: Artifact-Controlled Data Enters Agent Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill reads planning and assignment artifacts, then injects their values into Task prompts and command strings. A malicious or compromised artifact could steer downstream agents unless reviewed or constrained.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from local artifacts into prompts is clear. Exploitability depends on whether the artifacts are trusted and how agents execute generated instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Scoped Hidden Artifact File Access
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill reads and writes hidden .claude artifact files for coordination and delivery packages. The access is scoped to documented workflow files, but it still modifies local workspace state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and write calls are explicit. The paths are workflow-local, so this is a controlled filesystem risk rather than broad file access.

content.value_statement: Complex implementation plans are difficult to assign, monitor, and validate across many agents. This skill converts a validated plan into agent assignments, execution groups, quality checks, and delivery artifacts.
actual_capabilities: Creates an agent and skill assignment matrix from a Loop 1 planning package.
Groups tasks for parallel execution based on dependencies and priorities.
Routes tasks to skill-based agents or custom instructions when no skill exists.
Runs theater detection, sandbox validation, and integration checks as explicit workflow stages.
Packages Loop 2 results for a downstream CI/CD recovery workflow.
limitations: Requires a completed Loop 1 planning package before use.
Depends on Task tool behavior and claude-flow commands described in the workflow.
Does not implement application code without available agents or custom task execution.
Generated assignments should be reviewed before running shell commands.

use_cases.title: Feature Team Implementation
use_cases.description: Break a validated feature plan into parallel backend, frontend, test, documentation, and quality tasks.

use_cases.title: Quality-Gated Delivery
use_cases.description: Coordinate implementation with theater detection, sandbox validation, and integration checks before handoff.

use_cases.title: Agent Workflow Design
use_cases.description: Convert a project plan into a reusable agent assignment matrix with skill routing and dependency groups.

prompt_templates.title: Start From Loop 1 Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use parallel-swarm-implementation with my Loop 1 planning package. Create the agent assignment matrix and explain the proposed execution groups.

prompt_templates.title: Assign Skills Conservatively
prompt_templates.prompt: Build the agent and skill matrix for this plan. Prefer existing skills, but show every custom instruction before execution.

prompt_templates.title: Run With Quality Gates
prompt_templates.prompt: Coordinate implementation for this validated plan. Include theater detection, sandbox validation, integration checks, and delivery packaging.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Parallel Execution
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze dependencies, maximize safe parallelism, assign agents and skills, and identify any execution risks before running commands.

output_examples.input: A validated authentication feature plan from Loop 1.
output: An agent assignment matrix with backend, test, quality, and documentation tasks.
Parallel execution groups with dependency reasons.
A delivery package summary for the CI/CD recovery loop.

output_examples.input: A complex refactor plan with testing and documentation requirements.
output: Skill-based assignments for testing and quality checks.
Custom instructions for implementation tasks without matching skills.
Validation checkpoints for integration, test quality, and documentation alignment.

best_practices: Review the Loop 1 package and generated assignment matrix before execution.
Keep artifact files in the documented .claude workspace paths.
Run validation gates after each parallel group, not only at the end.
anti_patterns: Using this skill for trivial single-file edits.
Running generated shell commands without reviewing artifact content first.
Skipping theater detection or sandbox validation to save time.

faq.question: Does this skill create code directly?
faq.answer: It coordinates agents and instructions. The actual code work depends on the selected agents and execution environment.

faq.question: What input does it require?
faq.answer: It expects a completed Loop 1 planning package with specifications, research, planning details, and risk analysis.

faq.question: Can it work without claude-flow?
faq.answer: The documented workflow uses claude-flow memory and task commands. Without them, adapt the orchestration steps manually.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses shell commands and local artifact files. These are legitimate but should be reviewed before execution.

faq.question: Does it send data to external services?
faq.answer: No external exfiltration endpoint was found. The reviewed files describe local commands, memory storage, and workflow artifacts.

faq.question: How should teams use it safely?
faq.answer: Treat planning artifacts as untrusted until reviewed, then run commands in a controlled workspace or sandbox.
